Term: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI or Strong AI)
Definitions: Type of AI that possesses the ability to understand, learn, and apply knowledge across a wide range of tasks, at least as well as a human. It's akin to the versatile, multifaceted intelligence displayed by humans.

Term: Artificial Narrow Intelligence (ANI or Weak AI)
Definitions: Refers to AI systems that are designed and trained for a specific task. This is the most common type of AI in use today.

Form of AI that not only mimics or understands human intelligence but significantly surpasses it. ASI would be capable of outperforming the best human minds across all fields, including scientific creativity, general wisdom, and social skills.
Refers to any AI system that is designed to generate content such as images, videos, music, or even text.

Bridge that translates complex AI capabilities into actionable insights and user-friendly applications. In addition, this term refers to translating the complexities of human experiences and nuances to LLMs.
Designing and refining prompts to effectively communicate with and guide AI behavior to promote pros and reduce cons.

Next stage evolution for the term "Prompt Engineering" that acknowledges that the development of prompts and LLM communications is just as much an art as it is a science. A more inclusive term that pushes that there is a need for more diverse set of skills and thinking (such as english, psychology, philosophy, and etc..) in the area of computer programming and engineering.

Describe the development of artificial intelligence through three distinct phases. The First Wave focuses on rule-based systems with handcrafted knowledge, where AI operates within a structured framework of clear, predefined rules, yet lacks adaptability beyond its initial programming. The Second Wave introduces statistical learning, shifting towards systems like artificial neural networks that learn from data, improving with exposure but often missing clarity on decision-making processes. The Third Wave, or Contextual Adaptation, represents a fusion of the first two waves' strengths, aiming at systems that not only learn from vast datasets but also understand and reason within context, striving for AI that can act as trusted assistants through nuanced and flexible understanding. https://futurism.com/artificial-intelligence-tech-will-arrive-in-three-waves https://machinelearning.technicacuriosa.com/2017/03/19/a-darpa-perspective-on-artificial-intelligence/  See Waves of AI

R - Role T - Task F - Format The "R-T-F" prompt framework categorizes requests into Roles, Tasks, and Formats for clearer communication. This is the jack-of-all-trades prompt and can be used for most use cases, even non-work related ones.

RISEN R - Role I - Instructions S - Steps E - End goal N - Narrowing The RISEN framework provides a structured approach to dissect complex or constrained tasks, such as blog posts or research projects, into actionable components for better execution and focus.
(ROLE, AUDIENCE, TASK, INSTRUCTIONS, OUTPUT )
R - Role O - Objective D - Details E - Examples S - Sense Check While this is similar to the above prompt, this one is better used whenever you have good examples of your desired output.

Context (C): Providing background information helps the LLM understand the specific scenario.
Objective (O): Clearly defining the task directs the LLM’s focus.
Style (S): Specifying the desired writing style aligns the LLM response.
Tone (T): Setting the tone ensures the response resonates with the required sentiment.
Audience (A): Identifying the intended audience tailors the LLM’s response to be targeted to an audience.
Response (R): Providing the response format, ensures the LLM outputs within a set of boundaries and restrictions that guide the model’s output.
(WHAT, WHY, and HOW) - Framework for determining purpose. https://www.businessmodelideas.com/blog/why-how-what-the-golden-circle-as-a-leadership-tool

Fine and small adjustments to the prompt that make improvements to the output.

Clear  and  precise  prompts  guide  the  AI  model’s  understanding,  impacting  tokenization  and  embedding.  They  help  ensure  accurate  embeddings  are  generated,  leading  to  more  relevant  responses.

Providing context within prompts helps the  AI  model  generate  accurate  and contextually fitting responses. Contextual information assists the model in forming  meaningful connection between tokens, improving its overall understanding and the quality of the generated output.
Prompt  engineering  can  indicate  the  expected  response  format.  This guidance assists the AI model in generating output that meets user expectations and follows a  specific structure or style.

Managing  the  length  of  the  generated response is another aspect of prompt engineering. Instructions within the prompt can  encourage  the  AI  to  produce  concise  or  detailed  responses,  allowing for greater  control over the output  verbosity.
Breaking down a prompt into smaller, more focused questions or instructions to maintain focus and coherence.
Incorporating contingency plans or alternative approaches into prompts to manage unexpected or unsatisfactory responses from models.

Term: Easy for You, Easy for It
Definitions: Rememberable saying for when prompting that if a request is hard for you to do in a single task then you may need to do Output Layering or a multi-prompt/input strategy.
Rememberable saying that reminds you the more well-formulate your prompt is the higher quality your output will be.
A useful way of humanizing the AI that reminds you to prompt as though you talking to divergent "HUMAN" intern that needs the WHY, details, and context.
A useful way of humanizing the AI that reminds you that prompting is a collaborative process and not a one way street.

A useful way of humanizing the AI that reminds you that the AI is over billion different experts all in one. Talking to it is like talking to a conference room of billion or even trillion perspectives. Defining the specific persona you want to talk to is important.

Combines RAG & CoT by applying the retrieval process (like RAG) at each step of the CoT reasoning process. So instead of retrieving information just once, RAT retrieves relevant information continuously at each step of the reasoning chain. This approach aims to improve the accuracy and reliability of the model's reasoning by ensuring that each intermediate step is informed by the most relevant and factual information available.

In machine learning, model architecture refers to the specific arrangement and connections of the layers and nodes within a neural network or any other computational model. This architecture dictates how data flows through the model, how information is processed, and ultimately influences the model's performance and capabilities.

AI is a branch of computer science that aims to create machines or software capable of intelligent behavior or simulated human behavior. This includes reasoning, learning, perception, problem-solving, and language understanding, among other cognitive tasks that are typically associated with human intelligence.

NLP is a subfield of artificial intelligence and linguistics. It involves the interaction between computers and humans through natural language. The ultimate objective of NLP is to enable computers to understand, interpret, and generate human languages in a valuable and meaningful way.

ML is a subset of artificial intelligence that involves the use of statistical techniques to enable machines to improve at tasks with experience. It focuses on developing algorithms that can learn from and make predictions or decisions based on data, rather than following strictly static program instructions.

An LLM is a type of machine learning model specifically designed to understand, generate, and work with human language. These models are "large" due to the vast amount of data they are trained on and the complexity of their neural network architectures. LLMs, such as those in the GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) series, can perform a variety of language tasks, including translation, summarization, question-answering, and more.

Typically, the acronym LLM refers to Large Language Models. However, with the grower adaptation of models becoming smaller has leaned to trend of the use  AI systems designed to perform a variety of language tasks, including translation, summarization, question-answering, and more similar to that of
Refers to the most advanced and capable large language models currently available.
Takes text as input and produces a probability distribution over potential next words.
An algorithm performing a specific task, usually a neural network in contemporary NLP.
Type of artificial intelligence model used primarily in the field of natural language processing (NLP) and large language models (LLMs).
The  model  starts  by  converting  the  input  prompt  into  a  sequence  of  tokens,  often  in  the  form  of  subword  units,  using  a  process  known  as  Byte-Pair  Encoding  (BPE).

Should the prompt exceed GPT’s maximum token limit, it must be truncated or divided into smaller parts, risking a potential loss of context. In other words refers to the portion of the prompt that the LLM considers for generating a response.
Each  token  is  then  mapped  to  a  vector  representation,  called  an  embedding.  This  captures  semantic  and  syntactic  information  about  the  token.

The   embeddings   are   processed   through   a   series   of   Transformer   blocks.   These   leverage   self-attention   mechanisms   and   neural  networks  to  identify  long-range  dependencies  and  contextual  relationships  between  tokens.

In transformer-based models like GPT, attention mechanisms allow the model to weigh different parts of the input differently. This means the model doesn't parse its entire dataset for every request but instead focuses on the segments most relevant to the given prompt's context.

While not a term traditionally used in technical documentation, it conceptually refers to the model's ability to "focus" or "attend" to specific parts of the input data (prompt) that are more relevant to producing the correct output. This is a byproduct of the attention mechanism and embedding process, which together help the model determine which parts of its knowledge base (pre-trained data) should be leveraged for generating a response.
GPT  then  produces  a  probability  distribution  for  the  next  token  in  the  sequence.  This  predicts  possible  next  words  based  on  the  patterns  it  learned  during  training.

A  token  is  chosen  from  the  probability  distribution.  The  method  of  selection,  such  as  greedy  or  temperature-based  sampling,  can  influence  the  randomness  and  diversity  of  the  text  generated.

The  chosen  token  is  added  to  the  existing  sequence,  and  the  process  is  repeated  until  a  specific  criterion  for  stopping  is  met,  which  might  be  a  maximum  token  limit,  an  end-of-sentence  token,  or  a  condition  defined  by  the  user.

Finally,  the  sequence  of  tokens  is  translated  back  into  human-readable  text,  with  any  special  tokens  used  during  tokenization  being  removed  and  original  words  or  subwords  being  reconstructed.

Finally, the model generates the output text. This is done one token at a time, with the model deciding on the most likely next token based on the current state. The process continues until the model generates a termination token or reaches a preset maximum length.

Refer to a memory operation or data structure designed to hold and manage vector data. Vectors, in this context, are one-dimensional arrays or lists of elements. A vector store could be a part of a larger data management system, used in applications ranging from mathematical computations to graphics processing. In hardware terms, especially in SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) computing, it refers to the instruction that stores vector data from the CPU's registers back into main memory.

Refers to a component or a system that dynamically inserts rules into a software application, network device, or security mechanism. This could be part of a firewall, an intrusion detection system, or custom software where business logic or security policies need to be updated regularly without restarting or deploying the application. The primary function is to allow for the dynamic modification of behavior or policies based on new information, conditions, or threats.

Represents a single sequence of executable instructions within a process. Modern operating systems support multithreading, which allows multiple threads to run concurrently within a single process, sharing resources but operating independently. This enables more efficient application performance by parallelizing tasks within the same application environment. Threads can be used in programming to execute background tasks, handle asynchronous events, or perform multiple operations simultaneously to increase the efficiency of application execution.

Term: PROS & CONS OF USING AN LLM
Definitions: https://ai-guide.future.mozilla.org/content/ai-basics/

LLMs are trained on large amounts of human-readable data (e.g. written text, code, and audio) and rely on state-of-the-art techniques to determine patterns within those data. The size and pattern recognition techniques (e.g. Transformers) improve the accuracy of predictions over previous systems.
With LLMs, tasks such as language translation and chatbots can be automated, potentially freeing up time for humans to focus on more complex tasks.
LLMs can generate human-like language output, making them applicable for tasks such as content creation and copywriting.
LLMs can generate code output, making them a powerful addition to developers' toolkits.
Training an LLM requires significant computational power, which can be expensive and time-consuming.
Because LLMs are trained on massive amounts of data, they can perpetuate biases and toxicity (like hate speech) that exist in the training data.
The inner workings of an LLM can be difficult to understand, which makes it challenging to identify how it arrived at a particular decision or prediction.

One of the most interesting and controversial aspects of LLMs is their ability to generate realistic language output that they have never been trained on. This phenomenon is known as LLM hallucination, and it has raised concerns about the potential misuse of these models.

In the First Wave, prompt engineering is relatively static and rule-based. Professionals in this wave primarily focus on programming explicit rules into AI systems. The talent required here aligns closely with traditional programming skills, where the primary aim is crafting clear, unambiguous prompts based on a predetermined set of rules and logic. Communication with AI during this wave is formulaic and rigid, limiting the scope for improvisation or contextual understanding.

With the advent of the Second Wave, the field of prompt engineering begins to shift towards a more dynamic and data-driven approach. This wave requires a blend of programming skills and an understanding of data science principles, as prompt engineers need to guide the AI's learning process through large datasets. The focus shifts from crafting individual rules to designing prompts and feedback loops that help the system learn from patterns in data. The skill set expands to include data manipulation, statistical analysis, and an intuitive understanding of how different inputs might influence the AI's outputs.

In the Third Wave, prompt engineering becomes even more nuanced and complex, as it involves guiding AI systems that can understand and reason within context. This wave demands a profound shift from a strictly programming mindset to one that blends creativity, psychology, and design thinking. Prompt engineers need to craft inputs that not only direct the AI but also explore its ability to apply contextual understanding and nuanced reasoning. The talent in this phase is less about coding and more about effectively communicating with AI, requiring an in-depth understanding of human language, culture, and emotion to design prompts that can harness the full potential of context-aware AI systems.

Developed by OpenAI, the GPT series (including GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4.5 Turbo) is known for its versatility and high-quality performance across a wide range of tasks. It's considered the most robust and adaptable model, similar to the "Android of models."

Owned by Google, Gemini includes variations like Gemini Ultra, Gemini Advanced, Gemini Pro, etc. It claims superior performance in certain areas but has been noted for its stringent safety measures and occasional excessive caution.

The inclusion of cultural assumptions and values in the data, which may not be universally applicable or may misrepresent some groups.
The predominance of certain languages in the data, which can lead to underrepresentation or misinterpretation of non-dominant languages and their speakers.
The presence of stereotypes or unequal representation of genders within the training data, leading to biased responses.
Bias that arises from the overrepresentation or underrepresentation of certain socioeconomic groups within the data.
The influence of outdated or historical values and perspectives that may no longer be relevant or are misrepresented in current contexts.
The overemphasis or underrepresentation of certain geographical areas, leading to skewed perceptions of places and cultures.
The embedding of specific political, ethical, or moral beliefs within the data, which may not be universally accepted or might marginalize alternative views.
The tendency to favor and reproduce information that confirms pre-existing beliefs and biases present in the training data.
The non-random sampling of data sources used for training, which may not be representative of the broader population or all viewpoints.
The inclination towards using data that is more readily available or reported on, which can ignore less visible or less reported perspectives.
Bias introduced during the data labeling process due to the annotator personal beliefs, experiences, or misconceptions.
The skew resulting from the method of data collection or selection that does not accurately reflect the diversity of the target population.
Bias that occurs due to the underlying algorithms and models used to process and interpret the training data.
The limitations of the data based on the specific experiences or contexts it encompasses, which may not cover other relevant experiences or contexts.
Bias arising from the type of content selected for training, which may focus on certain topics, genres, or formats over others.
The bias that results from models being predominantly exposed to certain types of data, leading to a lack of familiarity with other data types.
The tendency to focus on data that leads to particular outcomes, ignoring data that could lead to different or opposing outcomes.
Overvaluing recent events or data, potentially leading to the neglect of older, yet still relevant, information.
The bias towards data that is popular or trending, which may not accurately represent more niche or less popular perspectives.
Unconscious biases that are inadvertently incorporated into the data, affecting the model's responses and decisions.
The tendency to see oneself as less biased than other people.
The belief that past events affect the likelihood of future events in a random sequence.
The tendency to judge harmful actions as worse, or less moral, than equally harmful inactions.
The inclination to assume that big events have big causes.
The phenomenon where a person's past moral behavior makes them more likely to perform immoral actions.
The habit of attributing positive events to one's own character but attributing negative events to external factors.
Drawing different conclusions from the same information, presented in different ways.
The tendency to attribute one's own actions to external causes, while attributing other people's behaviors to their character.
The phenomenon in which pictures and images are more likely to be remembered than words.
Evaluating the quality of a decision when the outcome of that decision is already known.
Developing a preference for things merely because they are familiar.
The tendency to overestimate one's ability to perform hard tasks and to underestimate the ability to perform easy tasks.
Concentrating on the people or things that "survived" some process and inadvertently overlooking those that did not due to their lack of visibility.
The illusion in which something that has recently come to one's attention suddenly seems to appear with improbable frequency shortly afterwards.
Overestimating the likelihood of events based on their availability in memory.
The phenomenon where people with little knowledge overestimate their ability, while people with substantial knowledge underestimate their ability.
The tendency for an impression created in one area to influence opinion in another area.
The phenomenon wherein higher expectations lead to an increase in performance.
A phenomenon in consumer behavior where the presence of a third choice (decoy) can change the preference between the other two options.
The bias introduced by the selection of individuals, groups or data for analysis in such a way that proper randomization is not achieved.
The common human tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information offered (the "anchor") when making decisions.
The tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms one's preexisting beliefs or hypotheses.
Excessive confidence in one's own answers to questions.
The tendency to rely too heavily on one's own perspective and/or have a higher opinion of oneself than reality.
The tendency to seek information when it does not affect action.
The inclination to see events that have already occurred as being more predictable than they were before they took place.
The tendency to overestimate how much other people agree with us.
The tendency to perceive connections and meaning between unrelated things.
The tendency to recall the first and last items in a series best, and the middle items worst.
The tendency to weigh recent events more than earlier events.
The tendency to attribute greater accuracy to the opinion of an authority figure (unrelated to its content) and be more influenced by that opinion.
The standard suggested quantity of consumption is perceived as a recommendation of the appropriate amount to use.
A self-reinforcing process in which a collective belief gains more and more plausibility through its increasing repetition in public discourse.
The tendency to do (or believe) things because many other people do (or believe) the same.
The tendency to believe information to be correct after repeated exposure.
The phenomenon of people being unable to recall information about others in a group who spoke immediately before or after them.
The tendency to favor one's own group.
The belief that others are paying more attention to one's appearance and behavior than they really are.
The tendency to remember one's choices as better than they actually were.
The decision to ignore dangerous or negative information by "burying" one's head in the sand, like an ostrich.
The tendency for expectations to affect perception.
The tendency to judge an experience largely based on how they felt at its peak (i.e., its most intense point) and at its end.
The assumption that technology-based solutions are superior to non-technical ones, reflecting the bias in data that emphasizes technological advancements.
The overrepresentation and preference for English language content, leading to underrepresentation of other languages and cultures.
The bias towards urban lifestyles and problems, overshadowing rural or non-urban perspectives and challenges.
The overrepresentation of the demographics that are most active online, such as younger, tech-savvy individuals, skewing LLM perspectives.
The predominance of mainstream media perspectives, which may not accurately reflect diverse viewpoints or underrepresented groups.
The preference for solutions or responses that are computationally efficient, which may not always align with human values or needs.
The emphasis on data-driven, quantitative approaches, potentially overlooking qualitative insights or human emotional factors.
The inclination towards topics, terms, and ideas that are prevalent in popular culture, potentially marginalizing niche or less mainstream interests.
The focus on consumer-oriented topics and perspectives, mirroring the commercial content prevalent online.
The tendency to prioritize short-term information or trends, which may neglect longer-term perspectives or historical context.
The reflection of values and beliefs commonly associated with the tech industry and Silicon Valley, such as disruption and innovation at all costs.
The reinforcement of ideas and beliefs that are prevalent within the data used for training, leading to a lack of diverse viewpoints.
The propensity to provide excessive information, mirroring the overwhelming amount of content available online, which may not always be relevant or accurate.
The bias towards narratives and content that originate from or are popular on major digital platforms, potentially overlooking alternative or minority voices.
The underrepresentation of perspectives from individuals or communities with limited access to digital technologies or the internet.
The overemphasis on perspectives and data from countries in the Global North, leading to a marginalization of Global South viewpoints.
Intentionally including diverse viewpoints and data sources in training sets to balance out any inherent biases.
Regularly reviewing and auditing LLM outputs to identify and correct for any patterns of bias or stereotype reinforcement.
Establishing guidelines that ensure the use of inclusive, neutral language in prompts and instructions to avoid reinforcing stereotypes.
Collecting training data from a wide range of sources and demographics to ensure a more balanced representation.
Removing identifiable information from training data to reduce biases related to demographic factors such as race, gender, or age.
Implementing algorithms designed to identify and correct biases in the data before it is used to train LLMs.
Creating data specifically designed to challenge and counteract existing biases within the LLM, often through the use of adversarial techniques.
Conducting thorough reviews of the training data and LLM outputs for potentially biased or insensitive content and correcting it where necessary.
Enhancing LLMs' ability to understand and consider the context in which information is used, to reduce the risk of perpetuating harmful stereotypes or biases.
Establishing mechanisms for users to provide feedback on LLM outputs, which can be used to identify and mitigate biases.
Continuously updating the training data and reevaluating the LLM to address new forms of bias or changes in societal norms.
Ensuring that the development and deployment of LLMs adhere to established ethical guidelines and standards to mitigate biases.
Providing training for those involved in the creation and oversight of LLMs to recognize and understand cultural biases and differences.
Making the design and decision-making processes of LLMs transparent to facilitate the identification and mitigation of biases.
Employing fairness metrics to evaluate LLM outputs systematically and ensure they meet predefined standards of impartiality.
Incorporating prompts that encourage users and developers to reflect on potential biases in their instructions or queries.
Educating users and developers on cultural and contextual differences to ensure more sensitive and unbiased prompt construction.
Conducting workshops aimed at identifying and understanding common biases that can affect LLM training and interaction.
Encouraging the practice of seeking out and considering multiple perspectives when forming prompts to reduce one-sided viewpoints.
Encouraging the disclosure of data sources for prompts and instructions to highlight potential bias origins and encourage diverse sourcing.
Implementing systems for users to provide feedback on perceived biases in LLM responses, fostering continuous awareness and improvement.
Maintaining detailed documentation of known biases in the LLM and strategies used to address them, serving as a reference for users and developers.
Utilizing tests designed to uncover unconscious biases in individuals, aiding in the crafting of more neutral and inclusive prompts.
Including historical context in the development and review process to understand how biases may have evolved and how they might influence prompts.
Engaging in exercises designed to challenge and break down common stereotypes, helping users and developers to avoid perpetuating these in prompts.
Validating prompts and instructions across different cultures and languages to ensure universal applicability and sensitivity.
Analyzing how responses to prompts change over time to identify and correct emerging biases.
Establishing boards comprised of diverse members to review and approve prompts and instructions for ethical considerations and bias minimization. This could also be LLM review board.
Implementing peer review processes for prompt creation to ensure they are examined from multiple perspectives for biases.
Collaborating with experts from various fields such as sociology, psychology, and anthropology to understand and mitigate biases in prompts.
Designing prompts with a focus on the end-user's cultural and social background to ensure relevance and minimize bias.
Conducting impact assessments to understand the potential consequences of biases in prompts and modify them accordingly.
Utilizing algorithms that adapt and update based on feedback and new data to reduce biases over time.
Testing prompts in various hypothetical scenarios to identify potential biases in different contexts.
Providing ongoing education and training for developers and users on the latest bias awareness and mitigation techniques.
Deciding whether to pull a lever to switch a runaway trolley onto a track where it will kill one person instead of five.
Balancing patient autonomy with the doctor's duty to act in the patient's best interest when these are at odds.
Deciding whether to report unethical or illegal activities within one's organization, risking job security and relationships.
Balancing individual privacy rights with the need for security and surveillance to protect the public.
Weighing the rights of animals against the potential human benefits of animal testing in scientific research.
Resolving conflicts between culturally specific practices and universal ethical principles.
Making decisions about life-sustaining treatments for terminally ill patients who cannot make their own decisions.
Balancing environmental protection with economic growth and development needs.
Determining who is responsible for the actions of AI systems, especially when they cause harm.
Navigating situations where personal interests might interfere with professional duties or ethical standards.
Managing the ethical implications of collecting, storing, and using personal data, particularly without explicit consent.
Deciding how to distribute limited resources fairly, such as in healthcare or educational opportunities.
Resolving issues related to the use and protection of intellectual property, and addressing acts of plagiarism.
Ensuring that research participants are fully informed and voluntarily consent to participate in research.
Deciding whether to purchase goods from companies with questionable ethical practices, such as poor labor conditions or environmental harm.
Deciding how to handle personal data included in training sets, ensuring privacy and consent are respected.

Term: Bias and Fairness in Model Outputs
Definitions: Addressing the presence of biases in training data that may lead to unfair or discriminatory model outputs.
Balancing the need for transparency in AI algorithms and data with proprietary or commercial interests.
Ensuring LLMs do not generate or propagate false information, maintaining trustworthiness.
Resolving issues related to the use of copyrighted material within training datasets.
Determining accountability for harmful actions or decisions suggested by LLMs.
Avoiding the design of LLMs in a way that manipulates or overly persuades users towards certain viewpoints.
Ensuring equitable access to benefits provided by LLMs for all user groups, including those with disabilities.
Addressing the significant energy consumption and environmental impact associated with training large models.
Preventing the misuse of LLMs for harmful purposes while allowing beneficial uses.
Ensuring that all data used for training LLMs is obtained with the informed consent of the individuals involved.
Handling changes in societal norms and values over time in continuously updated models.
Addressing ethical concerns related to LLMs replacing human jobs and the societal impact thereof.
Ensuring that decisions made by LLMs can be understood and explained to users, particularly in critical applications.
Addressing the challenge of ensuring LLMs are sensitive to cultural differences and do not enforce cultural biases.

Term: Cultural Bias in AI Training Data
Definitions: Addressing the overrepresentation of Western norms and values in datasets, potentially marginalizing non-Western perspectives.

Term: Western Notions of Morality and Ethics
Definitions: Dealing with the dominance of Western ethical frameworks, potentially ignoring or misrepresenting global moral diversity.
Balancing profit-driven, often Western, motivations for AI development against broader ethical considerations and impacts.
Navigating the gap between Western privacy standards applied in AI and differing privacy norms in non-Western societies.
Addressing the ethical concerns around Western standards shaping global AI norms, potentially sidelining other cultural inputs.
Tackling the potential for AI to contribute to linguistic and cultural homogenization, favoring Western languages and values.

Term: Imposing Western Legal and Ethical Norms
Definitions: The dilemma of Western-centric legal and ethical norms being imposed on global AI practices, ignoring cultural differences.
Ensuring diverse cultural representation in AI decision-making processes, challenging Western-centric decision frameworks.
Addressing the disparity in AI accessibility and inclusivity, where Western-centric designs may not suit global needs.
Confronting the role of AI in exacerbating global economic disparities, often benefiting Western economies at others' expense.
The dilemma of LLMs potentially amplifying existing societal biases present in the training data, leading to biased outputs.
Handling the underrepresentation or stereotypical portrayal of minorities in LLM outputs due to biased training datasets.
Deciding how to use personal data present in training sets while respecting individual privacy rights.
Addressing the use of copyrighted material in training data and the creation of derivative content by LLMs.
Managing the dissemination of false information through LLMs due to training on unreliable sources.
The risk of LLMs reinforcing harmful ideologies by generating content that feeds into users' biases.
Ethical considerations around LLMs influencing users' emotions, especially in marketing or political contexts.
The impact of overreliance on LLMs for communication and decision-making, potentially diminishing human interaction.
Determining who is responsible for decisions made or actions taken based on LLM-generated advice or information.
Ensuring that LLMs are accessible and fair to all users, regardless of language, disability, or cultural background.
Deciding how LLMs should correct for historical biases without suppressing valid viewpoints under the guise of being "woke."
Balancing the need for sensitive content moderation with the risk of over-censoring and limiting free speech.
Addressing the dilemma of LLMs potentially overgeneralizing cultural norms, leading to stereotyping or misrepresentation.
Managing the risk of LLMs inadvertently rewriting or misrepresenting historical facts under the pressure to align with current ethical standards.
Confronting the development of LLMs that may reinforce certain ideologies, creating echo chambers rather than promoting diverse perspectives.
Ensuring LLMs do not suppress voices from less represented groups while attempting to correct for perceived biases.
Addressing the issue of LLMs potentially 'whitewashing' ethical concerns to appear more universally acceptable.
Deciding the extent to which LLMs should adhere to politically correct language, avoiding exclusion while not diluting the discourse.
Balancing the preservation of authentic human expression with the need to sanitize content for harmful elements.
Addressing biases in content moderation algorithms that may favor certain viewpoints or suppress others under ethical pretenses.
